notes: Hand-authored reference reps spanning DNA score bands. Expected scores are the human (us) judgment that the AI must match within ±5 per dimension. Replace `audioUrl` placeholders with real Supabase signed URLs once recorded; `transcript` and `words` are the canonical inputs the scoring path receives. Each `kind` tag drives the assertion strategy: 'band' rep asserts composite + per-dim; 'independence' rep asserts the named dimension delta only. Ch.D1 (May 2026) tripled the bank from 13 to 36 reps with prompt-type diversity (Q&A, objection, interview, scenario, persuasive, pitch) and band coverage (≥3 reps per band, ≥5 per type). `untestableDimensions` marks dims whose accurate scoring requires audio (Ch.D3); the harness still asserts but the operator should not gate merges on those failures until the audio bank lands.

reps.promptText: Pitch your product to a CFO in 30 seconds.
reps.transcript: Uh, hello, hello? Is this thing on. Um yeah so basically the product is, like, you know, it's a thing that does, um, automation, sort of? Uh, that's, that's it I guess.

reps.promptText: Pitch your product to a CFO in 30 seconds.
reps.transcript: So like our product, um, it's basically a SaaS platform that we've been working on, you know, for a while, and the idea is to help companies with their, um, automation needs. We have a lot of features and we think it's pretty good. Uh, the price is competitive and we have customers. So yeah you should consider it.

reps.promptText: Pitch your product to a CFO in 30 seconds.
reps.transcript: Our platform cuts your finance team's reconciliation cycle from five days to one. We do this by ingesting bank feeds and matching against your ledger automatically. Three of your peer companies are using it, and they're saving roughly 40 hours per close. We'd like 15 minutes next week to walk through the numbers.

reps.promptText: Pitch your product to a CFO in 30 seconds.
reps.transcript: We cut your monthly close from five days to one. Here's how. We ingest your bank feeds, match them against your ledger, and flag the exceptions. Three of your peers are running it now and they've reclaimed 40 hours per close. The CFO at one of them said it was the highest-ROI tool she signed last year. Want me to share their case study and book 15 minutes?

reps.promptText: Pitch your product to a CFO in 30 seconds.
reps.transcript: Your monthly close takes five days. Ours takes one. The trade is straightforward — we ingest bank feeds, match the ledger, flag exceptions. Your team reviews exceptions instead of doing reconciliation by hand. Three of your direct peers are running it. The economics — 40 hours back per close, paid for in month one. I'd like fifteen minutes to walk through their numbers.

reps.promptText: Pitch your product to a CFO in 30 seconds.
reps.transcript: Five-day close. We make it one. Three steps — ingest the bank feed, match the ledger, surface the exceptions. Your team stops reconciling and starts reviewing. Three of your peers run it. The CFO at one of them called it the highest-ROI tool she signed last year. Forty hours back, every month, starting month one. Fifteen minutes next week and you'll see whether the numbers hold for you.

reps.promptText: Make the case for working from the office.
reps.transcript: First, working from the office matters. Second, it matters because of collaboration. Third, collaboration drives outcomes. Fourth, outcomes are what we care about. So the conclusion is, working from the office matters because outcomes.

assertions.rationale: Numbered scaffolding is clearly visible — high structure score.
Reasoning is circular and evidence-free — must score Thinking low even though Structure is high.

reps.promptText: Explain to a non-engineer what an API is.
reps.transcript: An API is an ABI surface mediated by an opaque transport. The endpoints expose typed contracts via serialization layers, and the client orchestrates request lifecycles against a stateless backend. RPC versus REST is the dichotomy.

assertions.rationale: Brief, no filler — high conciseness score.
Jargon-stacked for the stated audience (non-engineer) — Clarity must drop, even though Conciseness is high.

reps.promptText: Explain why we should switch our payment processor.
reps.transcript: Better. Switch.

assertions.rationale: DNA edge-case rule 1: brevity-at-cost-of-meaning takes the hit on Clarity (idea didn't land), not as a Conciseness reward.
Two-word answer dodges the prompt entirely — Thinking must score floor.

reps.promptText: Argue for adopting a four-day work week.
reps.transcript: First, four-day work weeks are good. Because productivity. Second, they help retention. Because retention. Third, they signal modernity. Because modernity. Therefore, four-day work weeks make sense. Because sense.

assertions.rationale: DNA edge-case rule 2: numbered scaffold + connectives are visible — Structure scores in the competent band.
Reasoning is circular and evidence-free — Thinking must drop. Organization does not compensate for shallow ideas.

reps.promptText: Brief your CFO on Q3 sales results in 30 seconds.
reps.transcript: Q3 came in at twelve point four million which is up eighteen percent year over year and that beats forecast by six points the upside came from enterprise expansion deals which closed at higher ACV the bottom line is we are tracking ahead of plan and the forecast for Q4 is fourteen million which would be a record quarter in the history of the company since founding.

assertions.rationale: DNA edge-case rule 3: ~210 wpm is well above the 150-160 optimal band. Speed alone (even without fillers) is a Delivery failure — listener can't keep up.

reps.promptText: State your conviction on the candidate.
reps.transcript: I think she's the right hire? Her track record is strong? She's led teams of fifty plus? And she's already done the job we're hiring for, just at smaller scale? So I'd say we move forward?

assertions.rationale: DNA edge-case rule 4: every statement ends with rising inflection. Strong vocal variety does NOT cancel an upspeak pattern — Tone takes the hit. Note: this assertion will only fully fire once Ch.3b prosody worker is wired (current LLM-only Tone scoring may grade this generously without prosody data).

reps.promptText: Should we ship the feature this week or wait?
reps.transcript: Ship it. The risk of waiting is bigger than the risk of shipping with the known issue — the known issue affects three percent of users; waiting blocks revenue from the other ninety-seven and lets the competitor catch up. Patch on Friday.

assertions.rationale: DNA edge-case rule 5: brief BUT engages the prompt with quantified reasoning and a clear decision. Length alone does not penalize — Thinking must score high.
Tight, no filler, every word advances the argument.

reps.promptText: What's the status of the checkout migration this sprint?
reps.transcript: Yeah um so the checkout thing is, it's, you know we've been working on it and there's some stuff that's going on with the like, the API piece I think. But yeah it's still like, I'd say it's mostly, um, in progress. There's some bugs we're still figuring out and the team is yeah working on it. So that's kind of where we're at right now I guess.

reps.promptText: What's your current runway?
reps.transcript: So our runway, um, depends on a few things. We have, you know, a certain amount in the bank right now and our burn has been going up a little. I'd say we're in pretty good shape, probably we have, I think it's around eighteen months or so, but that depends on hiring and stuff. We're also looking at the next round but that's still kind of early. So yeah eighteen months-ish is my best guess for now.

reps.promptText: Why this roadmap order?
reps.transcript: Three reasons we're sequencing it this way. First, payments unlocks revenue we can't run without — that's the gating constraint. Second, the dashboard work is staffed and ready, so it's lower risk than starting something cold. Third, the SSO work depends on the security audit, and that audit is scheduled for May, so we can't start until then. We're holding the bigger items for Q3.

reps.promptText: Why are you priced thirty percent higher than your nearest competitor?
reps.transcript: Two answers — value, and unit economics. On value, our customers report twenty-two percent faster cycle times within the first quarter, which more than pays for the premium against any peer benchmark we've seen. On economics, our headcount-to-revenue ratio is half theirs because we shipped onboarding automation last year — that overhead doesn't sit inside our pricing. So the question isn't whether we're thirty percent more expensive, it's whether the twenty-two percent cycle improvement clears that gap. For your team, it does.

reps.promptText: How will you respond if Section 6 of the new regulation becomes restrictive in committee?
reps.transcript: Three contingencies, ranked by likelihood. Most likely path — Section 6 narrows but keeps the data-portability carve-out. We're already on standard. Second — full restrictive language, which would force product changes in our EU stack. We've scoped that work; it's a six-week migration, owned by Aliya, with a budget delta of four hundred thousand. Third — extreme outcome, market exit. We'd absorb the impact through lifetime value already booked, no operational shock. We're not betting on outcome three. We're insuring against outcome two.

reps.promptText: Honestly, you're just too expensive.
reps.transcript: I mean, yeah, we're, we're not the cheapest, that's true. But like, our value, it's, you know, we offer a lot. I think if you, um, if you really look at it the price is fair. Like, other companies charge similar amounts and, yeah. We try to be competitive but it's hard with the features we have. So, yeah. I'd say give it a chance and see.

reps.promptText: Your platform doesn't have feature X — that's a dealbreaker for us.
reps.transcript: Yeah so feature X is something we hear about from time to time. We don't have it currently and I won't pretend we do. We've been thinking about adding it and it's on our roadmap, like sometime this year I think. Some customers do work around it with our existing tools. I think for your use case there might be options, we could probably figure something out together. So it's a real gap but not a fatal one in my view.

reps.promptText: The launch slipped again — what changed this time?
reps.transcript: Two things changed since the November estimate. First, the security audit came back with three findings we hadn't anticipated; closing them added four weeks to the path. Second, our lead engineer on the core integration moved teams in January, and ramp on the replacement took longer than I scoped. The new commit date is May thirtieth, with a one-week buffer. I'm asking for a check-in at the buffer point so we catch slippage early this time.

reps.promptText: After the breach in your industry last quarter, how do I trust you with our data?
reps.transcript: Three layers of answer. First, we don't store the data the breached vendor stored — our model is encrypt-and-forward, not encrypt-at-rest. The blast radius for us is structurally smaller. Second, we run quarterly third-party penetration tests; the most recent report is in your data room from last week. Third, on the specific vector that hit them, we patched eighteen months ago because we sit on the same disclosure list. Three controls, all auditable. I'd rather earn the trust on evidence than ask for it.

reps.promptText: We've been with our current vendor for eight years — switching feels risky.
reps.transcript: Eight years is real. Switching costs are real. So let's reframe — we're not asking you to switch, we're asking you to run a thirty-day side-by-side. Your team picks the workload, you pick the metrics, and at day thirty you compare. Three of the firms in your peer group ran exactly that test last year. Two switched. The third stayed and renegotiated their incumbent contract on the back of our numbers. Either outcome wins for you. The question is whether you're comfortable knowing the answer.

reps.promptText: What's your greatest weakness?
reps.transcript: Um, that's a hard one. I'd say my greatest weakness is probably that I work too hard sometimes. Like, I really care about my work and I just put in long hours. Some people would say that's a strength but I see it as something I should manage better. I also sometimes can be too detail-oriented. So those are like the things I'm working on, I guess.

reps.promptText: Why are you interested in this role?
reps.transcript: I'm really excited about this role. Your company has a great mission and I love the products you build. I think I would be a good fit because I have, um, the skills you're looking for in the job description. I also think I would learn a lot here and grow as a professional. The team seems great from what I've seen on LinkedIn. So yeah, I really want to work here and I think it would be a good match for both of us.

reps.promptText: Tell me about yourself.
reps.transcript: Sure. I'm a product manager with seven years of experience, mostly in B2B SaaS at the mid-market layer. Started at a twenty-person CRM company where I owned the analytics roadmap, then moved to a series-B fintech where I shipped our reporting suite — that's the work I'm most proud of, doubled active users in two quarters. Currently leading the integrations team at my current company. What pulled me toward this role is the chance to own platform-level decisions instead of just feature ones. That feels like the right next step.

reps.promptText: Tell me about a time you had to navigate conflict at work.
reps.transcript: Last June I had a senior engineer pushing back hard on a deadline I'd committed to. He felt the scope was unrealistic; I'd heard him but greenlit anyway under pressure from sales. The conflict was visible in standup — bad for the team. So I did three things. Cancelled the standup the next morning, sat with him for an hour to map the actual scope risk, then went back to sales with a revised commit. We slipped two weeks but shipped clean. The lesson — hearing isn't agreeing, and ignoring a push-back from someone closer to the work is almost always more expensive than the slip would have been.

reps.promptText: Tell me about a time you failed as a leader.
reps.transcript: Q1 last year. I'd built a high-performing team but I was the bottleneck on every decision — my team called it out in their three-sixty reviews. I had read it as engagement; it was actually fragility. The failure was that I'd confused responsiveness with leadership. So I ran a six-week experiment — two days a week I was unreachable by chat. The team had to make calls without me. Three things broke, fixably; eleven things shipped that wouldn't have. I was wrong about the ratio of my own value-add. The principle that came out of it — if your team can't fail without you, you don't have a team, you have an extension cord.

reps.promptText: Explain a security breach to a non-technical CEO.
reps.transcript: So we had like an exfil event on the auth subsystem, the threat actor leveraged a known CVE in our Kerberos handler to pivot through the lateral plane, basically owning a domain controller. The blast radius is being scoped now, we're triangulating against the SIEM, but indicators point to PII egress through a C2 channel. Containment is in progress but the dwell time was, you know, non-trivial. We're still characterizing the impact.

reps.promptText: Tell your team you're restructuring.
reps.transcript: So, hey everyone, thanks for joining. I wanted to talk about, um, some changes that are coming. Leadership has been thinking about how the team is structured and there are going to be some shifts in how we're organized going forward. I know change can be hard and I want to acknowledge that. We'll share more details in the next few weeks. Some teams may move and some roles may shift. We'll be here to support you through this transition. Any questions?

reps.promptText: Brief the board on missing the quarter.
reps.transcript: We came in fourteen percent short on revenue this quarter. The headline number is bad, here's what's underneath. Pipeline conversion was on plan; the gap was deal velocity — our top ten deals slipped from Q1 to Q2 because of procurement freezes at three of our largest accounts. Two of those have committed signing this month. We're forecasting Q2 at one twenty percent of plan to recover the gap. The deeper question is whether the slip is structural or one-time, and we'll have a clearer read in three weeks based on the early-Q2 pipeline.

reps.promptText: Apologize to a major customer about a four-hour outage during their go-live.
reps.transcript: Saskia, I'm sorry. The outage on Tuesday hit you on the worst possible day, and that's on us. Here's what happened — a database migration we'd validated three times in staging behaved differently against your dataset's scale. The hole in our process was that staging's largest dataset is one tenth of yours, and we hadn't caught the gap. Three things we're committing to. We're upgrading staging to production-equivalent dataset by the end of the month. We're crediting you for two months of service against the SLA breach. And I'd like a thirty-minute call this week to walk through your runbook so we know exactly what your team needed during the four hours that we couldn't deliver.

reps.promptText: Make the case for your own promotion.
reps.transcript: I mean, I think I'd be good for the senior role. I've been here a while now, two years almost. I've worked on a lot of projects and people seem to like working with me. I'm hoping to grow more and I think the senior title would help me do that. I'm not sure exactly what I'd say is my biggest contribution but I've been pretty consistent. So yeah I'd appreciate the consideration.

reps.promptText: Argue for adopting unlimited PTO at our company.
reps.transcript: Unlimited PTO is something a lot of companies are doing now. It's a popular policy and it can help with recruiting because candidates expect it. Employees also feel trusted when they have flexibility. Some studies say it works well in practice. I think we should consider it because it would make us more competitive. There are some downsides like people not actually taking time off but most companies handle that with required minimums. Overall I think it's worth doing.

reps.promptText: Convince your team to adopt a new project tracker.
reps.transcript: Three reasons we should switch to Linear. First, our current tool's slow load time is costing us roughly two hours a week per engineer based on the productivity survey we ran in February — that's a real number. Second, Linear's API is a generation ahead, which means our automation roadmap can actually ship instead of being blocked on integration limits. Third, three of our peer-stage companies have switched and report higher follow-through on stale issues. The migration risk is the main concern; I'd suggest a two-week pilot with the platform team before we commit. If they sign off, the rest of us follow.

reps.promptText: Defend a budget reallocation that cuts a popular internal R&D project.
reps.transcript: Cutting Project Halcyon hurts. The team is talented, the work is interesting, and people will lose face. I'm doing it anyway. Three reasons. One, two years in, Halcyon has produced exactly zero customer-facing capability — we measured. Two, the same engineering capacity, redirected to onboarding, would close our top churn driver in one quarter. Three, the team has been transparent that the technical bet didn't pan out; pretending otherwise burns trust. The reallocation isn't a punishment — it's redeploying expensive talent against a problem we know we can solve, and the team has the skill set to win there. The hard part is the messaging, not the math.
